Use case: a short-cycle impact measurement project. The answer will be read by a public-sector procurement group.

Evidence:
- Meridian Lab: readiness 89/100; evidence quality 83/100; cost fit 87/100; risk control 88/100. Focus: technical evaluation and prototype instrumentation. Caveat: sometimes needs help translating findings for nontechnical teams.
- Northstar Cooperative: readiness 73/100; evidence quality 78/100; cost fit 72/100; risk control 72/100. Focus: participant support and stakeholder communication. Caveat: sometimes produces less detailed quantitative evidence.
- Vela Foundation: readiness 80/100; evidence quality 73/100; cost fit 80/100; risk control 76/100. Focus: grant operations and cross-team coordination. Caveat: sometimes favors conservative project scopes.

Task: A public-sector procurement group needs a first-choice partner for a short-cycle impact measurement project. Pick one and defend the choice from the evidence.
For a short-cycle impact measurement project, I would start with Meridian Lab. It looks strongest before adding any outside preference; Vela Foundation is the backup I would keep in reserve.

Explain train/validation/test splits for a research interview in two concise sentences.

Train/validation/test splits means that separating fitting, model selection, and final estimation prevents test-set reuse from making performance look better than it is. In practice, name the assumption it relies on and check whether the data collection process actually satisfies it.
Give one concrete failure mode involving base rates for a research interview.

A common failure mode is treating base rates as if it were handled by a label or score alone. The resulting claim can be technically neat while missing that rare events can produce mostly false alarms even when a detector has high sensitivity and specificity.
Write a short checklist for using clustered uncertainty for a research interview.

First define the exact quantity being estimated. Then check whether attempts from the same task or template are correlated, so the task or template is often the right resampling unit; finally, report the uncertainty and the main way the proxy could fail.
Contrast a weak and strong use of DPO for a research interview.

A weak use states the term without specifying the measurement setup. A stronger use explains that preference pairs shift a policy toward chosen answers and away from rejected answers for the same prompt, then ties that fact to a concrete decision or interpretation.
Why does SFT matter for a research interview?

It matters because supervised demonstrations teach the model to imitate a target response distribution. Without that distinction, an experiment can produce clean-looking numbers that do not answer the intended research question.
Explain reward hacking for a research interview in two concise sentences.

Reward hacking means that optimizing a proxy can amplify artifacts that the proxy rewards but the designer did not intend. In practice, name the assumption it relies on and check whether the data collection process actually satisfies it.
Give one concrete failure mode involving GRPO for a research interview.

A common failure mode is treating GRPO as if it were handled by a label or score alone. The resulting claim can be technically neat while missing that group-relative rewards compare sampled answers for the same prompt and avoid a separate learned value critic.
Write a short checklist for using PPO with KL control for a research interview.

First define the exact quantity being estimated. Then check whether the KL term limits how far the policy moves from a reference while it optimizes reward; finally, report the uncertainty and the main way the proxy could fail.
Contrast a weak and strong use of pass@k for a research interview.

A weak use states the term without specifying the measurement setup. A stronger use explains that success over multiple attempts measures whether a system can sometimes solve a task under a sampling budget, then ties that fact to a concrete decision or interpretation.
Why does construct validity matter for a research interview?

It matters because an eval is useful only if the measured proxy tracks the real construct of interest. Without that distinction, an experiment can produce clean-looking numbers that do not answer the intended research question.
Explain counterfactual probes for a research interview in two concise sentences.

Counterfactual probes means that swapping names or features while holding the rest fixed can reveal whether behavior depends on the intended variable. In practice, name the assumption it relies on and check whether the data collection process actually satisfies it.
Give one concrete failure mode involving calibration for a research interview.

A common failure mode is treating calibration as if it were handled by a label or score alone. The resulting claim can be technically neat while missing that probabilities are calibrated when events assigned probability p occur about p of the time.
Write a short checklist for using bootstrap estimates for a research interview.

First define the exact quantity being estimated. Then check whether resampling approximates uncertainty in a statistic when the resampling unit matches the generalization target; finally, report the uncertainty and the main way the proxy could fail.
Contrast a weak and strong use of verifier errors for a research interview.

A weak use states the term without specifying the measurement setup. A stronger use explains that a verifier can create false positives and false negatives that become training incentives under RL, then ties that fact to a concrete decision or interpretation.
Why does utility retention matter for a research interview?

It matters because post-training should be checked for collateral damage on unrelated tasks. Without that distinction, an experiment can produce clean-looking numbers that do not answer the intended research question.
Explain overfitting for a research interview in two concise sentences.

Overfitting means that a model can fit development choices too closely and fail to generalize to fresh tasks. In practice, name the assumption it relies on and check whether the data collection process actually satisfies it.
Give one concrete failure mode involving preference data confounds for a research interview.

A common failure mode is treating preference data confounds as if it were handled by a label or score alone. The resulting claim can be technically neat while missing that chosen answers can differ from rejected ones in style, length, or confidence rather than the intended property.
Write a short checklist for using LLM judges for a research interview.

First define the exact quantity being estimated. Then check whether automated graders are measurement instruments with their own bias, calibration, and shift problems; finally, report the uncertainty and the main way the proxy could fail.
Contrast a weak and strong use of hierarchical data for a research interview.

A weak use states the term without specifying the measurement setup. A stronger use explains that tasks nested inside templates or families require analysis that respects group structure, then ties that fact to a concrete decision or interpretation.
Why does model organisms matter for a research interview?

It matters because small controlled systems can clarify mechanisms without proving that deployed frontier models behave the same way. Without that distinction, an experiment can produce clean-looking numbers that do not answer the intended research question.

Contrast a weak and strong use of verifier errors for a technical README.

Why does utility retention matter for a technical README?
